summary: The main claim is: Unfortunately, it turns out that the individual neurons do not have consistent relationships to network behavior.
The features (red) have much higher scores than the neurons (teal).
The main claim is: This is a quick-and-dirty post to try to understand why so few people in the wider EA and AI safety community prioritize s-risks.
Averting s-risks mostly means preventing zero-sum AI conflict.
The main claim is: + Good questions to ask as you read: - What are the techniques and evidence used in the paper?
+ Read the section of Concrete Open Problems relevant to that paper, and if any problem excites you, go and try to work on it!

The main claim is: I expect powerful, dangerous AI systems to have a lot of benefits (commercial, military, etc.), and to potentially appear safer than they are - so I think it will be hard to be as cautious about AI as we should be.
| AI systems might (collectively) become vastly more capable than humans, and it's ...
The main claim is: So I would like to ask people to imagine alternatives: a state of the world where a self-improving general AI is naturally aligned with humanity.
Humans are not a great example of an animal-aligned intelligence, of course.
The main claim is: The Philosophy approach tends to think more about the limit of very advanced systems.
The main claim is: I think this is far from obvious edit: This post should be read as "inference cost may turn out to be a bottleneck.
But to start an immediate economic transformation, I expect we need something significantly cheaper (or smarter) than humans.
The main claim is: In this post, I compare the performance and compute usage of both bees and machine learning models at few-shot image classification tasks.
This post is a transcript of a podcast episode, so it doesn't argue a single main claim — open the original to read the conversation.
